channel.title: tip on Daniels page
channel.description: Recent content in tip on Daniels page

item.title: Media Diet Dump
item.description:
• John Le Carré - The Pigeon Tunnel. Reading Carrè is always a pleasure. This one is suprisingly self-reflexive.
  • Matthias Adolfosn. Playfull and witty illustration. Can’t get enough of them.
  • Kosko - Neural Networks and Fuzzy Systems: An oldie. Intersting to get a grasp of the historical developement of artifical neural networks and fuzzy logic.
  • Song of the Sea. <3
  • Patterson. Don’t get fooled by the trailer. The movie is very slow; almost meditative. Watched it three times now, and i like it more with every viewing.
  • Youtube: Inverse Problems with TensorFlow. I don’t like fusion-power as a technology, but I like thinking about inverse problems. Pretty nice presentation!
  • Youtube: How to keep players engaged (without being evil). “Why do some games keep us rapt and entertained until the closing credits, while others fizzle out and end up on our pile of shame? Let’s look at some key ways developers can keep players interested (without being evil about it)”
  • Youtube: 1LIVE Chilly Gonzales - Pop Music Masterclass. Chilly Gonzales gives some intuitions about how pop-music works.
  • Youtube: Forging a knive from alufoil.
  • Youtube: Nerdwriter. The Renaissance Man of youtube.
  • Youtube: New Media Linguistics. Notes on language creation/invention, by David Peterson (Inventor of the Dorthraki language from GoT)

item.title: Using if else with pipes
item.description:
A nice way to use if else within pipes is (see here):

library(magrittr)
runif(1) %>% magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% {if(.) "foo" else "bar"}
## [1] "bar"

Alternatively, the when function from the purrr package can be used:

library(purrr, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
runif(1) %>% purrr::when(. <= 0.5 ~ "foo", 
                         . >  0.5 ~ "bar") 
## [1] "bar"

A nice way to use a vector of inputs is provided by the case_when function from dplyr together with mutate or transmute:

library(dplyr,  quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
library(tibble, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
runif(100) %>% 
  magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% 
  tibble::tibble(a = .) %>% 
  dplyr::transmute(., foobar = case_when(a <= 0.5 ~ "fu", a >  0.5 ~ "bar"))
## # A tibble: 100 x 1
##    foobar
##    <chr> 
##  1 fu    
##  2 fu    
##  3 bar   
##  4 fu    
##  5 fu    
##  6 fu    
##  7 bar   
##  8 bar   
##  9 fu    
## 10 fu    
## # ... with 90 more rows

item.title: Using if else with pipes
item.description:
A nice way to use if else within pipes is (see here):

library(magrittr)
runif(1) %>% magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% {if(.) "foo" else "bar"}
## [1] "bar"

Alternatively, the when function from the purrr package can be used:

library(purrr, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
runif(1) %>% purrr::when(. <= 0.5 ~ "foo", 
                         . >  0.5 ~ "bar") 
## [1] "bar"

A nice way to use a vector of inputs is provided by the case_when function from dplyr together with mutate or transmute:

library(dplyr,  quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
library(tibble, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
runif(100) %>% 
  magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% 
  tibble::tibble(a = .) %>% 
  dplyr::transmute(., foobar = case_when(a <= 0.5 ~ "fu", a >  0.5 ~ "bar"))
## # A tibble: 100 x 1
##    foobar
##    <chr> 
##  1 fu    
##  2 fu    
##  3 fu    
##  4 fu    
##  5 fu    
##  6 fu    
##  7 bar   
##  8 bar   
##  9 bar   
## 10 bar   
## # ... with 90 more rows

item.title: Feedback with tribbles
item.description:
The ordering and display of tables in Rmarkdown (aka data.frames or tibbles) has become so good. I actually prefer it over using print right now. Especially, because I can format in in an intuitive way by using the tribble() function. Here is an example. Let us say I want to summarize the basic set up for an optimization. Some dummy variables might look like this:

library(tibble)

number_iterations <- 1000
stoping_criterion <- -1
stepsize <- 0.5
sensitivity <- 0.2

To give a (nicely formatted) summary in the past I would have done something like this:

library(pasta)

print("number of iterations:" %&&% number_iterations)
## [1] "number of iterations: 1000"
print("stoping criterion:" %&&% stoping_criterion)
## [1] "stoping criterion: -1"
print("step size:" %&&% stepsize)
## [1] "step size: 0.5"
print("sensitivity:" %&&% sensitivity)
## [1] "sensitivity: 0.2"

Now, the tribble functions allows me to order the feedback in a way that is nicely formatted in code and in display:

info <- tribble(
  ~criterion,             ~value,
  "number of iterations", number_iterations,
  "stoping criterion",    stoping_criterion,
  "step size",            stepsize,
  "sensitivity",          sensitivity
)

info
## # A tibble: 4 x 2
##              criterion  value
##                  <chr>  <dbl>
## 1 number of iterations 1000.0
## 2    stoping criterion   -1.0
## 3            step size    0.5
## 4          sensitivity    0.2
